match: Brighton & Hove Albion Women vs Manchester City Women (Women's FA Cup Final 2026)
strat:
Brighton, under Dario Vidosic, typically employs an asymmetric 3-4-3 formation, which allows for balanced wide occupation and strong presence in central attacking areas. Their tactical approach emphasizes a 'vertical tiki-taka' style, focusing on quick interchanges and positional rotations to destabilize opponents. Defensively, they often transition into a 4-2-3-1, known for aggressive gegenpressing and a strong focus on winning back possession high up the pitch. They have proven to be a 'giant-killing machine' this season, notably securing a 3-2 victory against Manchester City in April.
Manchester City, led by Andrée Jeglertz, has evolved from a 4-3-3 to a more structured 4-2-3-1 system. Their attacking philosophy is direct, positive, and aggressive, with a high tempo aimed at exploiting dangerous pockets of space in the final third. The midfield features a double pivot, with Yui Hasegawa orchestrating play from deep, supported by another dynamic midfielder. Defensively, City adopts a gegenpressing model with player-to-player marking, often forming a mobile 4-4-2 shape, relying on the high work rate of their wingers. They recently clinched the Women's Super League title and are now aiming for a domestic double.
injuries:
For Brighton, key strikers Aisha Masaka (knee injury, out since July 2025) and Tahirah Heron (hamstring injury, out since November 2025) remain sidelined with long-term issues. Defender Maelys Mpome, who suffered a concussion in October 2025, has since made a full recovery and is available for selection.
Manchester City will be without defender Laura Wienroither and midfielder Iman Beney due to injuries. However, there is positive news regarding key winger Lauren Hemp, who has returned to full training and is confirmed to be available for the final. Additionally, Ayaka Yamashita, Rebecca Knaak, and Vivianne Miedema are all back in contention after missing City's final WSL match.
ref: The referee for the 2026 Women's FA Cup Final is Melissa Burgin, representing the Sheffield/Manchester FA. She was appointed to the FIFA International Referee List in 2025 and has extensive experience in women's football, having regularly officiated in the Barclays WSL. This marks her first time refereeing the Women's FA Cup Final, though she previously served as an assistant referee in the 2019 and 2021 finals. Her officiating style is generally regarded as modern, allowing the game to flow while maintaining firm control in high-stakes matches. The Video Assistant Referee (VAR) for the match will be Paul Howard.
tip: Manchester City enters this final as the favorites, having recently secured the WSL title and aiming for a domestic double. Their potent attacking lineup and well-drilled gegenpressing system under Jeglertz make them a formidable force. However, Brighton has demonstrated their ability to upset top teams, including a 3-2 victory over City earlier in the season, showcasing their tactical flexibility and the influence of experienced players like Fran Kirby. While Brighton will put up a strong fight, City's overall squad depth and winning momentum are likely to be decisive. A narrow victory for Manchester City, potentially with both teams finding the net, is the most probable outcome. Expect a high-intensity match with tactical adjustments from both sides.
prob:
Manchester City Win: 65% (Expected odds: 1.50)
Draw (after 90 mins): 20% (Expected odds: 4.00)
Brighton Win: 15% (Expected odds: 6.00)

strat:
Under Julian Nagelsmann, Germany typically employs a flexible 4-2-3-1 system that can fluidly transition into a 3-4-3 or 4-3-3 when in possession. Their tactical philosophy is characterized by high-intensity pressing, positional flexibility, and a strong emphasis on vertical play to quickly move the ball towards the goal. The team aims to control matches by pressing high and exploiting spaces through rapid transitions. Key players like Joshua Kimmich and Jamal Musiala are central to their ball progression and creative efforts. Germany has been in excellent form, boasting a seven-match winning streak.
Finland, managed by Jacob Friis since January 2025, is expected to adopt a compact defensive strategy, focusing on disciplined organization and quick counter-attacks. While they are considered the underdog in this fixture, Finland has shown significant improvement in recent years and is capable of surprising opponents with solid defensive performances. Their recent form includes a 2-0 victory against New Zealand and a 1-1 draw with Cape Verde in the FIFA Series in March 2026, indicating a resilient approach.
injuries:
Germany will be without several key players for this friendly. Manuel Neuer (goalkeeper) is sidelined with a minor calf problem and will not feature as a precautionary measure, with Oliver Baumann expected to start. Kai Havertz (forward) is unavailable due to his club Arsenal's participation in the Champions League final. Serge Gnabry (forward) has been ruled out of the upcoming World Cup due to an adductor tear and will also miss this match. Young Bayern teammate Lennart Karl is also in a race against time to regain fitness.
For Finland, defensive midfielder Adam Markhiev is recovering from a broken nose bone. Goalkeeper Lukas Hradecky, who recovered from a knee injury in time for the FIFA Series in March 2026, is expected to be available. Veteran striker Teemu Pukki has retired from international duty and will not be part of the squad.
ref: The referee for the Germany vs Finland friendly is Matheus Delgado Candançan from Brazil. This match marks his international debut as a FIFA referee, having received his badge in 2025. Candançan gained notoriety for issuing 23 red cards in a Campeonato Mineiro final in Brazil earlier this year, suggesting a very strict and potentially card-happy officiating style. His approach could lead to a fragmented game with frequent stoppages and a high number of disciplinary actions, which both teams will need to be mindful of.
tip: Germany, playing at home and in excellent form under Nagelsmann, is heavily favored to win this friendly. Despite the absence of some key players and Neuer resting, their tactical system, squad depth, and recent winning streak should be enough to secure a comfortable victory against Finland. Finland's disciplined defensive setup and potential for counter-attacks might pose some initial challenges, but Germany's high pressing and offensive creativity are expected to eventually break through. The referee's strict style could also play a role, potentially leading to more fouls and set-piece opportunities. Expect Germany to control possession and score multiple goals.
prob:
Germany Win: 80% (Expected odds: 1.20)
Draw: 15% (Expected odds: 5.00)
Finland Win: 5% (Expected odds: 10.00+)

strat:
Under Carlo Ancelotti, Brazil aims to play a more balanced and controlled attacking style, moving away from pure individual brilliance towards a stronger collective structure. They typically operate from a 4-3-3 or 4-2-3-1 base, which can transform into a 4-2-4 in possession, focusing on rapid wide attacks, quick combinations, and isolating defenders in one-v-one situations, particularly through players like Vinicius Jr. Defensively, Ancelotti emphasizes game management, midfield control, and preventing the team from being exposed during transitions, employing a selective high press.
Panama, coached by Thomas Christiansen, primarily uses a 3-4-2-1 formation in attack, which shifts to a 5-4-1 defensive shape when out of possession. They are known for their tactical flexibility, being comfortable with both maintaining possession against weaker opponents and executing quick vertical counter-attacks against stronger teams. Panama's strengths lie in its physicality, high intensity, and organized defensive structure, which helped them qualify for the World Cup unbeaten. Key midfielder Adalberto Carrasquilla is crucial for dictating tempo and linking play.
injuries:
Brazil faces significant injury concerns, with Neymar (forward) sidelined due to a grade two calf injury, expected to keep him out for two to three weeks. This rules him out of this friendly and casts doubt on his availability for the World Cup opener. Other important players like Rodrygo and Estêvão are also reported to be injured, which influenced Neymar's inclusion in the squad. Eder Militão is another key player currently out with an injury.
Panama will be without key midfielder Adalberto Carrasquilla, who sustained an injury in the Liga MX final and is expected to miss this friendly, though he aims to be fit for the World Cup. Fellow midfielder Aníbal Godoy is also out with a knock injury. Additionally, goalkeeper Luis Mejía is nursing an injury, meaning Orlando Mosquera is likely to start in goal.
ref: The referee for the Brazil vs Panama friendly is Daniel Schlager from Germany. He will be supported by German assistant referees Sven Washitzki-Günther and Lasse Koslowski, with Florian Badstübner serving as the fourth official. The Video Assistant Referee (VAR) team will consist of Christian Dingert and Katrin Rafalski. This refereeing appointment is part of an exchange program between the Brazilian Football Confederation (CBF) and the German Football Association. German referees are generally known for their adherence to the rules, professionalism, and a consistent approach to officiating, aiming to ensure fair play.
tip: Brazil, despite missing key attacking talents like Neymar, Rodrygo, and Estêvão, still possesses a squad with vastly superior individual quality and tactical depth under Carlo Ancelotti. Their refined attacking system and improved game management should allow them to dominate possession and create numerous scoring opportunities against Panama. Panama, while defensively organized and capable of counter-attacks, will be significantly weakened by the absence of key midfielders Adalberto Carrasquilla and Aníbal Godoy, which will hinder their ability to control the midfield and launch effective transitions. Expect a comfortable victory for Brazil, likely keeping a clean sheet, as they fine-tune their preparations for the World Cup.
prob:
Brazil Win: 85% (Expected odds: 1.10)
Draw: 10% (Expected odds: 6.00)
Panama Win: 5% (Expected odds: 12.00+)
